text: A small hole is created in the cystic duct, and a catheter is inserted to flush the ductal system with contrast dye. The dye is visible under fluoroscopy, providing an image of the hepatic duct system to detect any gallstones.

The surgeon places clips on the cystic duct to close the hole made for the cholangiogram, then cuts the remaining cystic duct and artery. Using electrocautery, the gallbladder is detached from its liver attachments. The surgical field is inspected for bleeding before the gallbladder is removed from the body.

The surgeon prepares to remove the gallbladder through an incision to the right of the belly button, using a retrieval bag to extract the organ intact. The incision may be enlarged if the gallbladder contains large or numerous stones.
